Grassland sanctuary in Churu — notified 1962; India's densest blackbuck (Antilope cervicapra) habitat outside Velavadar.
'Chhapar' means a flat saline depression — the sanctuary's unique ecosystem is a saline flat grassland.
Also Rajasthan's premier winter raptor site — hosts eastern imperial eagle, Tawny eagle, harriers and short-toed snake eagles in migration.
Summer max: 45-50°C (Phalodi recorded India's highest reliable air temp of 51.0°C on 19 May 2016).
Winter min: 0-3°C; frost is common in Churu.
Diurnal range often exceeds 20°C — the widest daily temperature swing of any Indian climate zone.
Summer max: 42-46°C; winter min: 3-8°C.
Churu regularly holds the state's twin records — highest summer AND lowest winter temperature in the same year.
The 'hot-summer semi-arid' zone anchors Rajasthan's outdoor evaporation industries — salt production and dyeing.
Jhalawar–Banswara–Pratapgarh — Rajasthan's mildest zone; summer max 38-42°C, winter min 10-14°C.
Monsoon-moderated temperatures; humidity remains 60-80% for four months a year.
Malwa plateau's black cotton soils (Vertisols) hold moisture through the winter, buffering diurnal swings.
Aravalli elevation moderates the arid extremes — summer max 38-42°C, winter min 8-12°C.
Mount Abu (Sirohi) is the coolest station in Rajasthan — summer max 32°C, winter min occasionally sub-zero.
The Sabarmati and Mahi headwater catchments sit in this zone — high rainfall on cool Aravalli slopes.
Covers ~60% of Rajasthan — the state's largest physiographic region.
Aeolian sand cover with longitudinal, star, and barchan dunes; playa lakes (rann) concentrate salt.
Elevation gradient from 100-300 m; Rajasthan's lowest point (Bap, Phalodi, ~100 m) sits here.
Aravalli-ridge sanctuary spanning Ajmer, Pali and Rajsamand — notified 1983.
Buffer to the Kumbhalgarh–Beawar Aravalli corridor; leopard, sloth bear and hyena populations.
Named for Colonel James Tod (1782-1835), the East India Company political agent who wrote 'Annals and Antiquities of Rajasthan'.
Rajasthan's only Muslim princely state — founded 1817 by Amir Khan Pindari with a British sanad.
Bisalpur Dam (Deoli tahsil, Tonk) on the Banas — Rajasthan's largest drinking-water reservoir; supplies Jaipur, Ajmer and Tonk via a 144-km pipeline.
Central Sheep and Wool Research Institute (Avikanagar, Malpura tahsil, Tonk) established 1962 — India's premier woolen-fibre research hub.
Home to Rasoolpura (Rani Sahiba Library / Maulana Azad Arabic-Persian Research Institute) — one of India's largest collections of Persian and Arabic manuscripts.
Rajmahal (Tonk) is one of Rajasthan's three triveni sangams — where Dai, Khari and Banas rivers meet.
Tonk district was Rajasthan's first to issue a family-photograph BPL card, and holds the Chauhan-era historic town of Uniara.
Founded 1559 by Maharana Udai Singh II after Chittor's third sack — the Mewar capital for the next four centuries.
Sits in the Girwa valley — a natural amphitheatre bowl in the southern Aravalli that made the site defensible.
'City of Lakes' — Pichola, Fateh Sagar, Swaroop Sagar, Rangsagar and Doodh Talai are five interconnected artificial lakes commissioned across five centuries.
Jaisamand Lake (built 1691, Maharana Jai Singh) — Rajasthan's second-largest artificial lake and one of the world's largest man-made lakes of its era.
Zawar (Girva tahsil, Udaipur) — Asia's largest lead-zinc mine complex and India's oldest known zinc-smelting site (12th c. distillation retorts, world-first industrial zinc production).
Home to IIM Udaipur (2011) — the only IIM in Rajasthan — and Rajasthan's oldest folk-culture research centre, Bharatiya Lok Kala Mandal (1952, Devi Lal Samar).
Bagor Haveli holds the world's largest turban (pagdi) — 850 m long, in the museum's collection.
Dholpur river-forest sanctuary — notified 1955, one of the oldest in Rajasthan.
Chambal Ravines habitat — chinkara, chausingha (four-horned antelope), blue bull.
Adjacent to the Ramsagar sanctuary; the two form the northern Chambal Sanctuary buffer.
Champion & Seth Type 5A — Southern Tropical Dry Deciduous; covers Aravalli piedmonts and Vindhyan slopes.
Key species: dhok (Anogeissus pendula), salar (Boswellia serrata), tendu, palash, mahua.
Ranthambore, Kumbhalgarh and Sariska tiger habitats are dry-deciduous.
Mixed thorn–deciduous ecotone across central Rajasthan (Jodhpur, Nagaur, Ajmer, Tonk).
Rohira (Tecomella undulata — 'marwar teak'), khejri, and neem dominate; dhok on rocky spurs.
The Rajasthan state flower (rohira) is native to this ecotone.
Tal Chhapar and Sonkhaliya (Ajmer) grasslands host India's densest blackbuck and Great Indian Bustard remnants respectively.
Dominant grasses: sewan (Lasiurus scindicus), dhaman (Cenchrus setigerus), murath — the Thar's key fodder base.
The Great Indian Bustard (Ardeotis nigriceps) — Rajasthan's state bird — survives only in these grasslands.
Champion & Seth Type 8A — Northern Subtropical Semi-Evergreen; only on Mount Abu (Sirohi).
Key species: pipal, banyan, banj oak, jamun, wild mango; Rajasthan's only orchid populations.
Habitat of the endemic Mount Abu skink (Eurylepis moerella) and India's westernmost leopard-native evergreen population.
Riverine forests along the Chambal, Banas, Mahi and Luni; jamun, arjun, khejri belts.
The National Chambal Sanctuary's forests are Rajasthan's largest surviving riparian tract.
Home to smooth-coated otter, mugger crocodile and gharial — riparian mammals and reptiles.
Champion & Seth Type 6B/C1 — Northern Tropical Thorn Forest; dominant in Barmer, Bikaner, Churu, Jaisalmer.
Key species: khejri (Prosopis cineraria — Rajasthan's state tree), rohira, kair, ker, phog, jaal.
Sacred groves ('orans') protect these forests around village common lands — Bhadria Oran (17,804 ha, Jaisalmer) is India's largest.
Fringing vegetation of Keoladeo, Sambhar, Pushkar, Bisalpur — reeds, sedges, water lilies, hydrilla.
Keoladeo hosts India's densest painted-stork breeding colony.
Rajasthan's Ramsar sites (Keoladeo 1981, Sambhar 1990, Khichan 2023, Menar 2023) protect four distinct wetland vegetation types.
Distinct from the Chambal-basin Banas — rises in the Aravalli at Sirohi and flows southwest into Gujarat's Little Rann.
One of only two Rajasthan rivers that reach the Arabian Sea; the other is the Mahi.
Its Gujarat lower course is dammed at Dantiwada — Rajasthan's contribution is monsoon flow only.

Karni Mata temple at Deshnoke, Bikaner — the 'rat temple' housing ~25,000 kabbas.
Sachiya Mata Temple at Osian, Jodhpur — 8th c. Pratihara sandstone shikhara.
Chittorgarh Fort — India's largest fort by area (280 hectares); UNESCO Hill Fort of Rajasthan.
Kumbhalgarh Fort — 36 km walls, world's second-longest continuous fortification.
Amber Fort, Amer — Kachhawaha capital before Jaipur (1727); UNESCO Hill Fort of Rajasthan.
Mehrangarh Fort, Jodhpur — Rao Jodha's 1459 seat of the Rathores, rising 122 m above the city.
Jaisalmer Fort — Rajasthan's only 'living fort', yellow-sandstone Bhati capital (1156).
